<code>
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

struct DSU {
    vll par, sz;
    DSU(ll n) {
        par.resize(n+1);
        sz.resize(n+1, 1);
        for (ll i = 1; i <= n; i++) par[i] = i;
    }
    ll find(ll x) {
        return par[x] == x ? x : par[x] = find(par[x]);
    }
    bool unite(ll a, ll b) {
        a = find(a); b = find(b);
        if (a == b) return false;
        if (sz[a] < sz[b]) swap(a, b);
        par[b] = a;
        sz[a] += sz[b];
        return true;
    }
};

void solve() {
    ll n, m1, m2;
    cin >> n >> m1 >> m2;
    
    DSU dsu1(n), dsu2(n);
    for (ll i = 0; i < m1; i++) {
        ll u, v; cin >> u >> v;
        dsu1.unite(u, v);
    }
    for (ll i = 0; i < m2; i++) {
        ll u, v; cin >> u >> v;
        dsu2.unite(u, v);
    }
    
    map<pll, ll> rep;
    for (ll v = 1; v <= n; v++) {
        ll r1 = dsu1.find(v);
        ll r2 = dsu2.find(v);
        if (rep.find({r1, r2}) == rep.end())
            rep[{r1, r2}] = v;
    }
    
    map<ll, set<ll>> row_to_cols;
    map<ll, set<ll>> col_to_rows;
    
    for (auto &p : rep) {
        ll r1 = p.first.first;
        ll r2 = p.first.second;
        row_to_cols[r1].insert(r2);
        col_to_rows[r2].insert(r1);
    }
    
    vector<pll> ans;
    
    queue<ll> q;
    for (auto &p : row_to_cols) {
        if (p.second.size() >= 2)
            q.push(p.first);
    }
    
    while (row_to_cols.size() > 1 && col_to_rows.size() > 1) {
        while (!q.empty()) {
            ll r = q.front();
            if (row_to_cols.find(r) == row_to_cols.end() || row_to_cols[r].size() < 2) {
                q.pop();
                continue;
            }
            break;
        }
        
        if (!q.empty()) {
            ll r1 = q.front();
            ll r2 = -1;
            for (auto &p : row_to_cols) {
                if (p.first != r1) {
                    r2 = p.first;
                    break;
                }
            }
            ll c1 = *row_to_cols[r1].begin();
            ll c2 = -1;
            for (ll c : row_to_cols[r1]) {
                if (c != c1) {
                    c2 = c;
                    break;
                }
            }
            if (row_to_cols[r2].find(c2) == row_to_cols[r2].end()) {
                for (ll c : row_to_cols[r2]) {
                    if (c != c1) {
                        c2 = c;
                        break;
                    }
                }
            }
            ll v1 = rep[{r1, c1}];
            ll v2 = rep[{r2, c2}];
            ans.pb({v1, v2});
            
            dsu1.unite(v1, v2);
            ll new_r = dsu1.find(v1);
            ll old_r = (new_r == r1) ? r2 : r1;
            
            dsu2.unite(v1, v2);
            ll new_c = dsu2.find(v1);
            ll old_c = (new_c == c1) ? c2 : c1;
            
            if (new_r != old_r) {
                if (row_to_cols[new_r].size() < row_to_cols[old_r].size())
                    swap(row_to_cols[new_r], row_to_cols[old_r]);
                for (ll c : row_to_cols[old_r]) {
                    row_to_cols[new_r].insert(c);
                    col_to_rows[c].erase(old_r);
                    col_to_rows[c].insert(new_r);
                }
                row_to_cols.erase(old_r);
            }
            if (new_c != old_c) {
                if (col_to_rows[new_c].size() < col_to_rows[old_c].size())
                    swap(col_to_rows[new_c], col_to_rows[old_c]);
                for (ll r : col_to_rows[old_c]) {
                    col_to_rows[new_c].insert(r);
                    row_to_cols[r].erase(old_c);
                    row_to_cols[r].insert(new_c);
                }
                col_to_rows.erase(old_c);
            }
            
            rep[{new_r, new_c}] = v1;
            rep.erase({old_r, old_c});
            rep.erase({new_r, old_c});
            rep.erase({old_r, new_c});
            
            if (row_to_cols[new_r].size() >= 2)
                q.push(new_r);
            for (auto &p : row_to_cols) {
                if (p.second.size() >= 2)
                    q.push(p.first);
            }
        } else {
            auto it = row_to_cols.begin();
            ll r1 = it->first;
            ll c1 = *it->second.begin();
            it++;
            ll r2 = it->first;
            ll c2 = *it->second.begin();
            ll v1 = rep[{r1, c1}];
            ll v2 = rep[{r2, c2}];
            ans.pb({v1, v2});
            
            dsu1.unite(v1, v2);
            ll new_r = dsu1.find(v1);
            ll old_r = (new_r == r1) ? r2 : r1;
            
            dsu2.unite(v1, v2);
            ll new_c = dsu2.find(v1);
            ll old_c = (new_c == c1) ? c2 : c1;
            
            if (new_r != old_r) {
                if (row_to_cols[new_r].size() < row_to_cols[old_r].size())
                    swap(row_to_cols[new_r], row_to_cols[old_r]);
                for (ll c : row_to_cols[old_r]) {
                    row_to_cols[new_r].insert(c);
                    col_to_rows[c].erase(old_r);
                    col_to_rows[c].insert(new_r);
                }
                row_to_cols.erase(old_r);
            }
            if (new_c != old_c) {
                if (col_to_rows[new_c].size() < col_to_rows[old_c].size())
                    swap(col_to_rows[new_c], col_to_rows[old_c]);
                for (ll r : col_to_rows[old_c]) {
                    col_to_rows[new_c].insert(r);
                    row_to_cols[r].erase(old_c);
                    row_to_cols[r].insert(new_c);
                }
                col_to_rows.erase(old_c);
            }
            
            rep[{new_r, new_c}] = v1;
            rep.erase({old_r, old_c});
            rep.erase({new_r, old_c});
            rep.erase({old_r, new_c});
            
            if (row_to_cols[new_r].size() >= 2)
                q.push(new_r);
            for (auto &p : row_to_cols) {
                if (p.second.size() >= 2)
                    q.push(p.first);
            }
        }
    }
    
    cout << ans.size() << '\n';
    for (auto &e : ans)
        cout << e.first << ' ' << e.second << '\n';
}

int main() {
    fastio;
    solve();
    return 0;
}

reference.think:
Okay so we have two forests on the same n nodes, each initially with some edges. We can add the same edge to both forests simultaneously, as long as neither forest gets a cycle. The goal is to maximize the number of edges added. n ≤ 10^5, time limit 2s.

The approach: compute for each vertex its degree in the union of both forests — that is, deg_union[v] = deg_mocha[v] + deg_diana[v]. Then sort all unordered vertex pairs (u,v) by deg_union[u] + deg_union[v] ascending, prioritizing pairs with smallest combined degree. For each pair, check if adding (u,v) would create a cycle in either forest by running DFS from u to see if v is already reachable. If no cycle in both, add the edge to both forests and update degrees. Keep going through the sorted list until no more edges can be added.

Let me trace Example 2: n=5, m1=3, m2=2.
Mocha edges: (5,4), (2,1), (4,3). Diana edges: (4,3), (1,4).

Mocha forest: 5-4-3 and 2-1. Components: {5,4,3}, {2,1}.
Diana forest: 4-3 and 1-4. Components: {1,4,3}, {2}, {5}.

Union degrees:
Node 1: deg_mocha=1 (edge 2-1), deg_diana=1 (edge 1-4) → deg_union=2
Node 2: deg_mocha=1, deg_diana=0 → deg_union=1
Node 3: deg_mocha=1 (4-3), deg_diana=1 (4-3) → deg_union=2
Node 4: deg_mocha=2 (5-4,4-3), deg_diana=2 (4-3,1-4) → deg_union=4
Node 5: deg_mocha=1, deg_diana=0 → deg_union=1

All unordered pairs sorted by deg_union[u]+deg_union[v]:
(2,5): 1+1=2
(1,2): 2+1=3
(2,3): 1+2=3
(3,5): 2+1=3
(1,5): 2+1=3
(1,3): 2+2=4
(2,4): 1+4=5
(3,4): 2+4=6
(4,5): 4+1=5
(1,4): 2+4=6

That's valid even though d1 is in M2 as well? Wait, condition for edge is that the two vertices are in different D-roots. d1=1 and d2=2 are different. So it's okay if d1 is also present in M2. The important thing is that we can pick a vertex from M1 with D-root d1, and a vertex from M2 with D-root d2, with d1 ≠ d2. That only requires that there exists d2 in M2 that is not equal to d1? Actually, we need d2 ∈ set(M2) and d1 ∈ set(M1), and d1 ≠ d2. It does *not* require that d1 ∉ set(M2). So my earlier requirement of both set differences non-empty was too strict. The correct condition: There exist d1 ∈ set(M1) and d2 ∈ set(M2) such that d1 ≠ d2. That's equivalent to set(M1) ≠ set(M2) or |set(M1) ∪ set(M2)| ≥ 2. In other words, the union of their D-sets has at least two distinct D-roots. Then I can pick d1 from set(M1) and d2 from set(M2) with d1 ≠ d2. If set(M1) = set(M2) and that set has size 1, then I cannot pick different d's.

So algorithm: While there are at least two M-roots and at least two D-roots, pick two M-roots a and b. If the union of their D-sets has size ≥ 2, then pick d1 from set(a), d2 from set(b) with d1 ≠ d2. Choose vertices from (a,d1) and (b,d2). Add edge. Merge a and b in M-DSU, merge d1 and d2 in D-DSU. Update structures.

If for all pairs of M-roots, the union of their D-sets has size 1 (i.e., every M-root has the same single D-root), then all vertices share the same D-root, contradicting that there are at least two D-roots. So such a pair must exist. Similarly, we could symmetrically pick two D-roots and find a pair of M-roots.

Thus, we can always find a valid edge until min(C_m-1, C_d-1) is reached.

Let's apply this corrected condition to Example 3 after the first few merges. After adding (1,2),(1,3),(1,4), we had M-roots: M1 (big), M5, M6, M8. D-roots: D1 (big), D7, D8. Pick M1 and M5: their D-sets: M1 has {D1, D7} (since it contains vertices with D1 and vertex 7 with D7), M5 has {D1}. Union size = 2 (D1 and D7). So pick d1 = D7 from M1, d2 = D1 from M5. Vertices: (M1, D7) -> vertex 7. (M5, D1) -> vertex 5. Add edge (7,5). That's valid: M1≠M5, D7≠D1. Merge M1 and M5 -> M1. Merge D7 and D1 -> D1. Now D-sets update. Continue similarly.

This yields a sequence similar to the example. The exact edges may differ but count will be max.

aha! — so the core is maintaining the bipartite view between M-components and D-components, and the invariant that as long as there are at least two of each, there exists a pair of components whose union of D-sets (or M-sets) has size at least two, guaranteeing a cross-edge. The efficient implementation uses DSUs with sets of cross-roots, and each merge updates those sets, always picking a pair from the current "active" components. The greedy of always connecting to vertex 1 failed because it didn't consider the need to keep both partitions sufficiently diverse; this method dynamically chooses pairs that maintain the ability to continue.

finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to determine the maximum number of edges that can be added to both Mocha's and Diana's forests such that the resulting graphs remain forests and the added edges are identical in both forests. Let's break down the steps: ### Step 1: Understand the Problem Constraints - Both forests start as trees (connected acyclic graphs). - We need to add the same set of edges to both forests. - The final graphs should also be forests (i.e., no cycles). ### Step 2: Identify Possible Edges to Add Since we need to maintain the forest property, we can only add edges that connect components. Specifically: - For each component in Mocha's forest, we can add an edge connecting it to another component in Mocha's forest. - Similarly, for Diana's forest, we can add an edge connecting its components. However, since we need to add the same edges to both forests, the possible edges to add are those that exist in both forests' component connectivity patterns. ### Step 3: Compute Component Connectivity For each forest, compute the connected components using DFS or BFS. Then, for each component, identify potential candidates to connect it to other components within the same forest. ### Step 4: Find Common Potential Edges Identify the common potential edges that can be added to both forests without creating cycles. This involves checking which edges exist in both forests' component connectivity patterns. ### Step 5: Greedily Select Edges Select edges greedily while ensuring no cycle formation. A simple greedy approach could be to pick edges that connect smaller components to larger ones, but we need to ensure that the selected edges do not form cycles in either forest. ### Step 6: Implement the Solution Let's implement this step-by-step in C++. #include #include #include #include using namespace std; void dfs(const vector>& adj, vector& visited, int node) { queue q; q.push(node); visited[node] = true; while (!q.empty()) { int current = q.front(); q.pop(); for (int neighbor : adj[current]) { if (!visited[neighbor]) { visited[neighbor] = true; q.push(neighbor); } } } } vector> find_components(const vector>& adj, int n) { vector visited(n + 1, false); vector> components; for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) { if (!visited[i]) { vector comp; dfs(adj, visited, i); for (int j = 1; j <= n; ++j) { if (visited[j]) { comp.push_back(j); } } components.push_back(comp); } } return components; } vector> get_component_edges(const vector>& adj, const vector>& components) { vector> component_edges(components.size()); for (const auto& edge : adj) { int u = edge[0], v = edge[1]; for (size_t i = 0; i < components.size(); ++i) { if (components[i].count(u) && components[i].count(v)) { component_edges[i].insert(edge[0]); component_edges[i].insert(edge[1]); break; } } } return component_edges; } bool is_valid_edge(const vector>& component_edges, size_t u_comp, size_t v_comp, int u, int v) { if (u_comp == v_comp) { return component_edges[u_comp].find(u) != component_edges[u_comp].end() && component_edges[v_comp].find(v) != component_edges[v_comp].end(); } else { return true; } } int main() { ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int n, m1, m2; cin >> n >> m1 >> m2; vector> mocha_adj(n + 1); vector> diana_adj(n + 1); for (int i = 0; i < m1; ++i) { int u, v; cin >> u >> v; mocha_adj[u].push_back(v); mocha_adj[v].push_back(u); } for (int i = 0; i < m2; ++i) { int u, v; cin >> u >> v; diana_adj[u].push_back(v); diana_adj[v].push_back(u); } auto mocha_components = find_components(mocha_adj, n); auto diana_components = find_components(diana_adj, n); vector> mocha_component_edges = get_component_edges(mocha_adj, mocha_components); vector> diana_component_edges = get_component_edges(diana_adj, diana_components); unordered_set used_edges; vector> result; for (size_t i = 0; i < mocha_components.size(); ++i) { for (size_t j = 0; j < diana_components.size(); ++j) { if (i == j) { for (int u : mocha_components[i]) { for (int v : mocha_components[j]) { string edge_str = to_string(min(u, v)) + "-" + to_string(max(u, v)); if (used_edges.count(edge_str) == 0 && is_valid_edge(mocha_component_edges, i, j, u, v) && is_valid_edge(diana_component_edges, i, j, u, v)) { used_edges.insert(edge_str); result.emplace_back(u, v); } } } } else { for (int u : mocha_components[i]) { for (int v : diana_components[j]) { string edge_str = to_string(min(u, v)) + "-" + to_string(max(u, v)); if (used_edges.count(edge_str) == 0 && is_valid_edge(mocha_component_edges, i, j, u, v) && is_valid_edge(diana_component_edges, i, j, u, v)) { used_edges.insert(edge_str); result.emplace_back(u, v); } } } } } } cout << result.size() << '\n'; for (const auto& edge : result) { cout << edge.first << ' ' << edge.second << '\n'; } return 0; }

To solve this problem, we need to determine the maximum number of edges that can be added to both Mocha's and Diana's forests such that the resulting graphs remain forests and the added edges are identical in both forests. Let's break down the steps:

### Step 1: Understand the Problem Constraints
- Both forests start as trees (connected acyclic graphs).
- We need to add the same set of edges to both forests.
- The final graphs should also be forests (i.e., no cycles).

### Step 2: Identify Possible Edges to Add
Since we need to maintain the forest property, we can only add edges that connect components. Specifically:
- For each component in Mocha's forest, we can add an edge connecting it to another component in Mocha's forest.
- Similarly, for Diana's forest, we can add an edge connecting its components.

However, since we need to add the same edges to both forests, the possible edges to add are those that exist in both forests' component connectivity patterns.

### Step 3: Compute Component Connectivity
For each forest, compute the connected components using DFS or BFS. Then, for each component, identify potential candidates to connect it to other components within the same forest.

### Step 4: Find Common Potential Edges
Identify the common potential edges that can be added to both forests without creating cycles. This involves checking which edges exist in both forests' component connectivity patterns.

### Step 5: Greedily Select Edges
Select edges greedily while ensuring no cycle formation. A simple greedy approach could be to pick edges that connect smaller components to larger ones, but we need to ensure that the selected edges do not form cycles in either forest.

### Step 6: Implement the Solution
Let's implement this step-by-step in C++.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
There are $$$m$$$ people living in a city. There are $$$n$$$ dishes sold in the city. Each dish $$$i$$$ has a price $$$p_i$$$, a standard $$$s_i$$$ and a beauty $$$b_i$$$. Each person $$$j$$$ has an income of $$$inc_j$$$ and a preferred beauty $$$pref_j$$$.

A person would never buy a dish whose standard is less than the person's income. Also, a person can't afford a dish with a price greater than the income of the person. In other words, a person $$$j$$$ can buy a dish $$$i$$$ only if $$$p_i \leq inc_j \leq s_i$$$.

Also, a person $$$j$$$ can buy a dish $$$i$$$, only if $$$|b_i-pref_j| \leq (inc_j-p_i)$$$. In other words, if the price of the dish is less than the person's income by $$$k$$$, the person will only allow the absolute difference of at most $$$k$$$ between the beauty of the dish and his/her preferred beauty.

Print the number of dishes that can be bought by each person in the city.

Time limit: 2s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
The first line contains two integers $$$n$$$ and $$$m$$$ ($$$1 \leq n \leq 10^5$$$, $$$1 \leq m \leq 10^5$$$), the number of dishes available in the city and the number of people living in the city.

The second line contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$p_i$$$ ($$$1 \leq p_i \leq 10^9$$$), the price of each dish.

The third line contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$s_i$$$ ($$$1 \leq s_i \leq 10^9$$$), the standard of each dish.

The fourth line contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$b_i$$$ ($$$1 \leq b_i \leq 10^9$$$), the beauty of each dish.

The fifth line contains $$$m$$$ integers $$$inc_j$$$ ($$$1 \leq inc_j \leq 10^9$$$), the income of every person.

The sixth line contains $$$m$$$ integers $$$pref_j$$$ ($$$1 \leq pref_j \leq 10^9$$$), the preferred beauty of every person.

It is guaranteed that for all integers $$$i$$$ from $$$1$$$ to $$$n$$$, the following condition holds: $$$p_i \leq s_i$$$.

Output:
Print $$$m$$$ integers, the number of dishes that can be bought by every person living in the city.

Note:
In the first example, the first person can buy dish $$$2$$$, the second person can buy dishes $$$1$$$ and $$$2$$$ and the third person can buy no dishes.

In the second example, the first person can buy no dishes, the second person can buy dishes $$$1$$$ and $$$4$$$, and the third person can buy dishes $$$1$$$, $$$2$$$ and $$$4$$$.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    3 3
    2 1 3
    2 4 4
    2 1 1
    2 2 3
    1 2 4
    Output:
    1 2 0

  Example 2:
    Input:
    4 3
    1 2 1 1
    3 3 1 3
    2 1 3 2
    1 1 3
    1 2 1
    Output:
    0 2 3

<think>
Okay so we have n dishes and m people, each with price, standard, beauty for dishes and income, preferred beauty for people. The constraints are n,m ≤ 10^5, all values up to 10^9. Time limit 2s, so budget roughly 2×10^8 ops. We need to count for each person how many dishes satisfy: p_i ≤ inc_j ≤ s_i AND |b_i - pref_j| ≤ inc_j - p_i.

The brute-force approach: for each person j from 1 to m, iterate through all dishes i from 1 to n, check the three conditions directly, increment a counter if all pass.

Let me trace Example 1. n=3 dishes, m=3 people.
Dishes:
  i=1: p=2, s=2, b=2
  i=2: p=1, s=4, b=1
  i=3: p=3, s=4, b=1
People:
  j=1: inc=2, pref=1
  j=2: inc=2, pref=2
  j=3: inc=3, pref=4

Person j=1 (inc=2, pref=1):
  i=1: p=2 ≤ inc=2 ≤ s=2 ✓. |b-pref| = |2-1|=1, inc-p=0. Is 1 ≤ 0? No. Fail.
  i=2: p=1 ≤ inc=2 ≤ s=4 ✓. |1-1|=0 ≤ 2-1=1 ✓. Pass. count=1.
  i=3: p=3 ≤ inc=2? No, 3 > 2. Fail.
  Result for j=1: 1. Matches output.

Person j=2 (inc=2, pref=2):
  i=1: p=2 ≤ 2 ≤ 2 ✓. |2-2|=0 ≤ 0 ✓. Pass. count=1.
  i=2: p=1 ≤ 2 ≤ 4 ✓. |1-2|=1 ≤ 1 ✓. Pass. count=2.
  i=3: p=3 ≤ 2? No. Fail.
  Result: 2. Matches output.

Person j=3 (inc=3, pref=4):
  i=1: p=2 ≤ 3 ≤ 2? No, 3 > s=2. Fail.
  i=2: p=1 ≤ 3 ≤ 4 ✓. |1-4|=3 ≤ 3-1=2? 3 ≤ 2? No. Fail.
  i=3: p=3 ≤ 3 ≤ 4 ✓. |1-4|=3 ≤ 3-3=0? 3 ≤ 0? No. Fail.
  Result: 0. Matches output.

So the brute-force gives correct answers on Example 1. Now let me check Example 2 quickly.
Dishes: n=4
  i=1: p=1, s=3, b=2
  i=2: p=2, s=3, b=1
  i=3: p=1, s=1, b=3
  i=4: p=1, s=3, b=2
People: m=3
  j=1: inc=1, pref=1
  j=2: inc=1, pref=2
  j=3: inc=3, pref=1

Person j=1 (inc=1, pref=1):
  i=1: p=1 ≤ 1 ≤ 3 ✓. |2-1|=1 ≤ 0? No. Fail.
  i=2: p=2 ≤ 1? No. Fail.
  i=3: p=1 ≤ 1 ≤ 1 ✓. |3-1|=2 ≤ 0? No. Fail.
  i=4: p=1 ≤ 1 ≤ 3 ✓. |2-1|=1 ≤ 0? No. Fail.
  Result: 0. Matches output.

Person j=2 (inc=1, pref=2):
  i=1: p=1 ≤ 1 ≤ 3 ✓. |2-2|=0 ≤ 0 ✓. Pass. count=1.
  i=2: p=2 ≤ 1? No.
  i=3: p=1 ≤ 1 ≤ 1 ✓. |3-2|=1 ≤ 0? No.
  i=4: p=1 ≤ 1 ≤ 3 ✓. |2-2|=0 ≤ 0 ✓. Pass. count=2.
  Result: 2. Matches.

Person j=3 (inc=3, pref=1):
  i=1: p=1 ≤ 3 ≤ 3 ✓. |2-1|=1 ≤ 2 ✓. Pass. count=1.
  i=2: p=2 ≤ 3 ≤ 3 ✓. |1-1|=0 ≤ 1 ✓. Pass. count=2.
  i=3: p=1 ≤ 3 ≤ 1? No, 3 > s=1. Fail.
  i=4: p=1 ≤ 3 ≤ 3 ✓. |2-1|=1 ≤ 2 ✓. Pass. count=3.
  Result: 3. Matches.

So brute-force is correct on both examples. Now the constraint check: n ≤ 10^5, m ≤ 10^5. The nested loop does n×m iterations. At maximum, 10^5 × 10^5 = 10^10 comparisons. Each comparison involves a few integer operations and branching. Time limit is 2 seconds. Typical budget for 2s in C++ is around 2×10^8 simple operations. 10^10 is 50× over that budget.

wait — 10^10 comparisons is 50× over the 2×10^8 budget. Even with early exits or branch prediction, the structural gap is two orders of magnitude.

LEARNING: Nested loop over all dish-person pairs is O(n×m)=10^10 comparisons, 50× over the 2×10^8 budget for 2s.

I'll sort dishes by p_i and people by inc_j. Two pointers: one adds dishes to an active set when p_i ≤ inc_j, another removes dishes when s_i < inc_j. For each person, I need to count active dishes where |b_i - pref_j| ≤ inc_j - p_i. The range depends on p_i per dish, so I can't use a single range query. I'll store active dishes in a list keyed by b_i, and for each person scan the active set linearly, checking the condition.

Let me trace Example 1:
Dishes: 
  d1: p=2, s=2, b=2
  d2: p=1, s=4, b=1
  d3: p=3, s=4, b=1
People:
  p1: inc=2, pref=1
  p2: inc=2, pref=2
  p3: inc=3, pref=4

Sort dishes by p: d2(p=1), d1(p=2), d3(p=3)
Sort people by inc: p1(inc=2), p2(inc=2), p3(inc=3)

Process p1 (inc=2, pref=1):
  Add dishes with p_i ≤ 2: d2(p=1), d1(p=2). Active = {d2, d1}.
  Remove dishes with s_i < 2: d1 has s=2, not <2, keep. d2 has s=4, keep. Active stays {d2, d1}.
  Scan active:
    d2: p=1, b=1. inc-p = 1. |b-pref| = |1-1| = 0 ≤ 1 → count.
    d1: p=2, b=2. inc-p = 0. |2-1| = 1 ≤ 0? No.
  Count = 1. Output for p1: 1. Correct.

Process p2 (inc=2, pref=2):
  Add dishes with p_i ≤ 2: already added d2, d1. Active = {d2, d1}.
  Remove dishes with s_i < 2: none. Active = {d2, d1}.
  Scan active:
    d2: p=1, b=1. inc-p = 1. |1-2| = 1 ≤ 1 → count.
    d1: p=2, b=2. inc-p = 0. |2-2| = 0 ≤ 0 → count.
  Count = 2. Output for p2: 2. Correct.

Process p3 (inc=3, pref=4):
  Add dishes with p_i ≤ 3: add d3(p=3). Active = {d2, d1, d3}.
  Remove dishes with s_i < 3: d1 has s=2 < 3 → remove d1. Active = {d2, d3}.
  Scan active:
    d2: p=1, b=1. inc-p = 2. |1-4| = 3 ≤ 2? No.
    d3: p=3, b=1. inc-p = 0. |1-4| = 3 ≤ 0? No.
  Count = 0. Output for p3: 0. Correct.

Trace Example 2:
Dishes:
  d1: p=1, s=3, b=2
  d2: p=2, s=3, b=1
  d3: p=1, s=1, b=3
  d4: p=1, s=3, b=2
People:
  p1: inc=1, pref=1
  p2: inc=1, pref=2
  p3: inc=3, pref=1

Sort dishes by p: d1(p=1), d3(p=1), d4(p=1), d2(p=2)
Sort people by inc: p1(inc=1), p2(inc=1), p3(inc=3)

Process p1 (inc=1, pref=1):
  Add dishes p_i ≤ 1: d1, d3, d4. Active = {d1, d3, d4}.
  Remove s_i < 1: none (all s_i ≥ 1). Active = {d1, d3, d4}.
  Scan:
    d1: p=1, b=2. inc-p=0. |2-1|=1 ≤ 0? No.
    d3: p=1, b=3. inc-p=0. |3-1|=2 ≤ 0? No.
    d4: p=1, b=2. inc-p=0. |2-1|=1 ≤ 0? No.
  Count = 0. Output p1: 0. Correct.

Process p2 (inc=1, pref=2):
  Add: same active {d1, d3, d4}.
  Remove: none. Active = {d1, d3, d4}.
  Scan:
    d1: p=1, b=2. inc-p=0. |2-2|=0 ≤ 0 → count.
    d3: p=1, b=3. inc-p=0. |3-2|=1 ≤ 0? No.
    d4: p=1, b=2. inc-p=0. |2-2|=0 ≤ 0 → count.
  Count = 2. Output p2: 2. Correct.

Process p3 (inc=3, pref=1):
  Add p_i ≤ 3: add d2(p=2). Active = {d1, d3, d4, d2}.
  Remove s_i < 3: d3 has s=1 < 3 → remove d3. Active = {d1, d4, d2}.
  Scan:
    d1: p=1, b=2. inc-p=2. |2-1|=1 ≤ 2 → count.
    d4: p=1, b=2. inc-p=2. |2-1|=1 ≤ 2 → count.
    d2: p=2, b=1. inc-p=1. |1-1|=0 ≤ 1 → count.
  Count = 3. Output p3: 3. Correct.

The approach works on both examples. Now constraints: n ≤ 10^5, m ≤ 10^5. For each person, I scan the active set. In the worst case, all n dishes are active for every person. That's m × n = 10^5 × 10^5 = 10^10 scans. Time limit 2s, budget ≈ 2×10^8 ops. 10^10 is 50× over budget.

wait — the active set scan is O(n) per person, total O(n×m)=10^10, 50× over the 2×10^8 budget.

LEARNING: Linear scan of active dishes per person gives O(n×m)=10^10 ops, 50× over the 2×10^8 budget.

I'll sort dishes by p_i and people by inc_j, then do divide-and-conquer on the income axis. The recursion splits the set of dishes and people into left/right halves based on a pivot income value. In the conquer step, I process dishes from the left half (p_i ≤ pivot) against people from the right half (inc_j > pivot), which guarantees p_i ≤ inc_j. The remaining conditions are inc_j ≤ s_i and the beauty constraint.

Let me rewrite the beauty condition: |b_i - pref_j| ≤ inc_j - p_i. This expands to pref_j - (inc_j - p_i) ≤ b_i ≤ pref_j + (inc_j - p_i). So for a fixed person j, the allowed b_i range is [pref_j - inc_j + p_i, pref_j + inc_j - p_i]. Notice p_i appears inside the bounds — the range depends on the specific dish's price, not just the person. That's the core difficulty.

In the conquer step, I have a set of dishes from the left half and people from the right half. For each person j, I need to count dishes i such that inc_j ≤ s_i AND b_i falls in that p_i-dependent range. I could try to process dishes grouped by p_i value. For each distinct p_i value, I'd maintain a Fenwick tree (or segment tree) over b_i coordinates, but b_i values are up to 10^9, so I'd need coordinate compression on all b_i values across all dishes. That's doable — compress all b_i and also the query bounds.

But the query bounds depend on p_i. For a person with income inc_j and preferred beauty pref_j, the condition for a dish with price p_i is: b_i must be between L(p_i) = pref_j - inc_j + p_i and R(p_i) = pref_j + inc_j - p_i. These bounds shift with p_i. If I group dishes by p_i, then for each p_i group I query the Fenwick tree for that group with the specific L and R for that p_i. But p_i can be up to 10^9 with n=10^5 dishes, so there could be up to 10^5 distinct p_i values. For each person (up to 10^5), I'd query up to O(number of distinct p_i in left half) Fenwick trees. In the worst case, the left half has O(n) distinct p_i values, and each person queries all of them. That's O(n × m) again across the recursion — the divide-and-conquer doesn't reduce the pairwise comparisons if I query every p_i group per person.

Let me trace Example 1 to see if there's a way to batch these queries.

Example 1:
Dishes (n=3):
  i=1: p=2, s=2, b=2
  i=2: p=1, s=4, b=1
  i=3: p=3, s=4, b=1

People (m=3):
  j=1: inc=2, pref=1
  j=2: inc=2, pref=2
  j=3: inc=3, pref=4

Sort dishes by p: dish2(p=1,s=4,b=1), dish1(p=2,s=2,b=2), dish3(p=3,s=4,b=1).
Sort people by inc: person1(inc=2,pref=1), person2(inc=2,pref=2), person3(inc=3,pref=4).

Now divide-and-conquer. Suppose pivot income = 2. Left half: dishes with p ≤ 2 (dish2, dish1), people with inc ≤ 2 (person1, person2). Right half: dishes with p > 2 (dish3), people with inc > 2 (person3). Conquer step: left dishes vs right people. Left dishes: dish2(p=1,s=4,b=1), dish1(p=2,s=2,b=2). Right person: person3(inc=3,pref=4).

<code>
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

struct Event {
    ll row, col, type, id;
    ll val;
};

void solve() {
    ll n, m;
    cin >> n >> m;
    vll p(n), s(n), b(n);
    for (ll i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> p[i];
    for (ll i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> s[i];
    for (ll i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> b[i];
    vll inc(m), pref(m);
    for (ll i = 0; i < m; i++) cin >> inc[i];
    for (ll i = 0; i < m; i++) cin >> pref[i];

    vector<Event> events;
    for (ll i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        ll P_row = p[i];
        ll P_col = b[i];
        ll s_row = s[i];
        events.pb({P_row, P_col, 0, -1, 1});
        events.pb({s_row + 1, b[i] - s[i] - 1 + p[i], 0, -1, -1});
        events.pb({P_row, P_col + 1, 1, -1, -1});
        events.pb({s_row + 1, b[i] + s[i] + 1 - p[i], 1, -1, -1});
    }

    for (ll j = 0; j < m; j++) {
        events.pb({inc[j], pref[j], 2, j, 0});
    }

    vll larr_coords, rarr_coords;
    for (auto &e : events) {
        if (e.type == 0) {
            larr_coords.pb(e.row + e.col);
        } else if (e.type == 1) {
            rarr_coords.pb(e.col - e.row);
        }
    }
    for (auto &e : events) {
        if (e.type == 2) {
            larr_coords.pb(e.row + e.col);
            rarr_coords.pb(e.col - e.row);
        }
    }
    sort(all(larr_coords));
    larr_coords.erase(unique(all(larr_coords)), larr_coords.end());
    sort(all(rarr_coords));
    rarr_coords.erase(unique(all(rarr_coords)), rarr_coords.end());

    auto get_lidx = [&](ll val) -> ll {
        return lower_bound(all(larr_coords), val) - larr_coords.begin() + 1;
    };
    auto get_ridx = [&](ll val) -> ll {
        return lower_bound(all(rarr_coords), val) - rarr_coords.begin() + 1;
    };

    ll L = sz(larr_coords);
    ll R = sz(rarr_coords);
    vll lbit(L + 2, 0), rbit(R + 2, 0);
    auto add_l = [&](ll idx, ll val) {
        for (; idx <= L; idx += idx & -idx) lbit[idx] += val;
    };
    auto sum_l = [&](ll idx) {
        ll s = 0;
        for (; idx > 0; idx -= idx & -idx) s += lbit[idx];
        return s;
    };
    auto add_r = [&](ll idx, ll val) {
        for (; idx <= R; idx += idx & -idx) rbit[idx] += val;
    };
    auto sum_r = [&](ll idx) {
        ll s = 0;
        for (; idx > 0; idx -= idx & -idx) s += rbit[idx];
        return s;
    };

    sort(all(events), [](const Event &a, const Event &b) {
        if (a.row != b.row) return a.row < b.row;
        return a.type < b.type;
    });

    vll ans(m, 0);
    for (auto &e : events) {
        if (e.type == 0) {
            ll idx = get_lidx(e.row + e.col);
            add_l(idx, e.val);
        } else if (e.type == 1) {
            ll idx = get_ridx(e.col - e.row);
            add_r(idx, e.val);
        } else {
            ll l_idx = get_lidx(e.row + e.col);
            ll r_idx = get_ridx(e.col - e.row);
            ans[e.id] = sum_l(l_idx) + sum_r(r_idx);
        }
    }

    for (ll i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        cout << ans[i] << (i + 1 == m ? '\n' : ' ');
    }
}

int main() {
    fastio;
    solve();
    return 0;
}

Okay so we have n dishes and m people, each with price, standard, beauty for dishes and income, preferred beauty for people. The constraints are n,m ≤ 10^5, all values up to 10^9. Time limit 2s, so budget roughly 2×10^8 ops. We need to count for each person how many dishes satisfy: p_i ≤ inc_j ≤ s_i AND |b_i - pref_j| ≤ inc_j - p_i.

The brute-force approach: for each person j from 1 to m, iterate through all dishes i from 1 to n, check the three conditions directly, increment a counter if all pass.

Let me trace Example 1. n=3 dishes, m=3 people.
Dishes:
  i=1: p=2, s=2, b=2
  i=2: p=1, s=4, b=1
  i=3: p=3, s=4, b=1
People:
  j=1: inc=2, pref=1
  j=2: inc=2, pref=2
  j=3: inc=3, pref=4

Person j=1 (inc=2, pref=1):
  i=1: p=2 ≤ inc=2 ≤ s=2 ✓. |b-pref| = |2-1|=1, inc-p=0. Is 1 ≤ 0? No. Fail.
  i=2: p=1 ≤ inc=2 ≤ s=4 ✓. |1-1|=0 ≤ 2-1=1 ✓. Pass. count=1.
  i=3: p=3 ≤ inc=2? No, 3 > 2. Fail.
  Result for j=1: 1. Matches output.

Person j=2 (inc=2, pref=2):
  i=1: p=2 ≤ 2 ≤ 2 ✓. |2-2|=0 ≤ 0 ✓. Pass. count=1.
  i=2: p=1 ≤ 2 ≤ 4 ✓. |1-2|=1 ≤ 1 ✓. Pass. count=2.
  i=3: p=3 ≤ 2? No. Fail.
  Result: 2. Matches output.

Person j=3 (inc=3, pref=4):
  i=1: p=2 ≤ 3 ≤ 2? No, 3 > s=2. Fail.
  i=2: p=1 ≤ 3 ≤ 4 ✓. |1-4|=3 ≤ 3-1=2? 3 ≤ 2? No. Fail.
  i=3: p=3 ≤ 3 ≤ 4 ✓. |1-4|=3 ≤ 3-3=0? 3 ≤ 0? No. Fail.
  Result: 0. Matches output.

So brute-force is correct on both examples. Now the constraint check: n ≤ 10^5, m ≤ 10^5. The nested loop does n×m iterations. At maximum, 10^5 × 10^5 = 10^10 comparisons. Each comparison involves a few integer operations and branching. Time limit is 2 seconds. Typical budget for 2s in C++ is around 2×10^8 simple operations. 10^10 is 50× over that budget.

wait — 10^10 comparisons is 50× over the 2×10^8 budget. Even with early exits or branch prediction, the structural gap is two orders of magnitude.

LEARNING: Nested loop over all dish-person pairs is O(n×m)=10^10 comparisons, 50× over the 2×10^8 budget for 2s.

I'll sort dishes by p_i and people by inc_j. Two pointers: one adds dishes to an active set when p_i ≤ inc_j, another removes dishes when s_i < inc_j. For each person, I need to count active dishes where |b_i - pref_j| ≤ inc_j - p_i. The range depends on p_i per dish, so I can't use a single range query. I'll store active dishes in a list keyed by b_i, and for each person scan the active set linearly, checking the condition.

I'll sort dishes by p_i and people by inc_j, then do divide-and-conquer on the income axis. The recursion splits the set of dishes and people into left/right halves based on a pivot income value. In the conquer step, I process dishes from the left half (p_i ≤ pivot) against people from the right half (inc_j > pivot), which guarantees p_i ≤ inc_j. The remaining conditions are inc_j ≤ s_i and the beauty constraint.

Let me rewrite the beauty condition: |b_i - pref_j| ≤ inc_j - p_i. This expands to pref_j - (inc_j - p_i) ≤ b_i ≤ pref_j + (inc_j - p_i). So for a fixed person j, the allowed b_i range is [pref_j - inc_j + p_i, pref_j + inc_j - p_i]. Notice p_i appears inside the bounds — the range depends on the specific dish's price, not just the person. That's the core difficulty.

In the conquer step, I have a set of dishes from the left half and people from the right half. For each person j, I need to count dishes i such that inc_j ≤ s_i AND b_i falls in that p_i-dependent range. I could try to process dishes grouped by p_i value. For each distinct p_i value, I'd maintain a Fenwick tree (or segment tree) over b_i coordinates, but b_i values are up to 10^9, so I'd need coordinate compression on all b_i values across all dishes. That's doable — compress all b_i and also the query bounds.

But the query bounds depend on p_i. For a person with income inc_j and preferred beauty pref_j, the condition for a dish with price p_i is: b_i must be between L(p_i) = pref_j - inc_j + p_i and R(p_i) = pref_j + inc_j - p_i. These bounds shift with p_i. If I group dishes by p_i, then for each p_i group I query the Fenwick tree for that group with the specific L and R for that p_i. But p_i can be up to 10^9 with n=10^5 dishes, so there could be up to 10^5 distinct p_i values. For each person (up to 10^5), I'd query up to O(number of distinct p_i in left half) Fenwick trees. In the worst case, the left half has O(n) distinct p_i values, and each person queries all of them. That's O(n × m) again across the recursion — the divide-and-conquer doesn't reduce the pairwise comparisons if I query every p_i group per person.

finetuned.full: To solve this problem efficiently within the given constraints, we need to determine for each person how many dishes they can buy based on their income and preferred beauty. The key constraints are: 1. A dish \( i \) can be bought by person \( j \) if \( p_i \leq \text{income}_j \leq s_i \). 2. Additionally, \( |b_i - \text{pref}_j| \leq (\text{income}_j - p_i) \). Given the large ranges (up to \( 10^5 \) dishes and \( 10^5 \) people), a direct nested loop approach would be too slow. We need a more efficient method. ### Steps to Solve: 1. **Sort Dishes**: Sort the dishes based on their prices \( p_i \). This allows us to process them in increasing order of price. 2. **Sliding Window**: For each person, use a sliding window over the sorted list of dishes to count how many dishes satisfy both conditions simultaneously. For each person \( j \): - Initialize a counter for valid dishes. - Iterate through the sorted list of dishes using two pointers or a sliding window technique. - Maintain a set of valid dishes that meet the initial condition \( p_i \leq \text{income}_j \leq s_i \). - Adjust the window as needed to ensure the additional condition \( |b_i - \text{pref}_j| \leq (\text{income}_j - p_i) \) is met. ### Implementation Details: 1. Read input values. 2. Sort the dishes by their price \( p_i \). 3. For each person: - Initialize counters. - Use a sliding window to count valid dishes. - Adjust the window when moving to the next person. This approach ensures that we efficiently filter dishes for each person without redundant checks. #include #include #include using namespace std; struct Dish { int price, standard, beauty; }; bool compareDish(const Dish& a, const Dish& b) { return a.price < b.price; } int solve(int n, vector& dishes, vector& incomes, vector& prefs) { sort(dishes.begin(), dishes.end(), compareDish); vector result(m); for (int j = 0; j < m; ++j) { int inc = incomes[j]; int pref = prefs[j]; int left = 0, right = 0; while (right < n) { while (right < n && dishes[right].price <= inc && dishes[right].standard >= inc) { if (abs(dishes[right].beauty - pref) <= (inc - dishes[right].price)) { right++; } else { break; } } while (left < right && (dishes[left].price > inc || dishes[left].standard < inc)) { left++; } if (left < right) { result[j]++; } } } return 0; } int main() { ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int n, m; cin >> n >> m; vector dishes(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { cin >> dishes[i].price >> dishes[i].standard >> dishes[i].beauty; } vector incomes(m), prefs(m); for (int i = 0; i < m; ++i) { cin >> incomes[i] >> prefs[i]; } solve(n, dishes, incomes, prefs); for (int i = 0; i < m; ++i) { cout << result[i] << " "; } cout << endl; return 0; }

- Initialize a counter for valid dishes.
- Iterate through the sorted list of dishes using two pointers or a sliding window technique.
- Maintain a set of valid dishes that meet the initial condition \( p_i \leq \text{income}_j \leq s_i \).
- Adjust the window as needed to ensure the additional condition \( |b_i - \text{pref}_j| \leq (\text{income}_j - p_i) \) is met.

### Implementation Details:

1. Read input values.
2. Sort the dishes by their price \( p_i \).
3. For each person:
   - Initialize counters.
   - Use a sliding window to count valid dishes.
   - Adjust the window when moving to the next person.

This approach ensures that we efficiently filter dishes for each person without redundant checks.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
You have a permutation: an array $$$a = [a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n]$$$ of distinct integers from $$$1$$$ to $$$n$$$. The length of the permutation $$$n$$$ is odd.

You need to sort the permutation in increasing order.

In one step, you can choose any prefix of the permutation with an odd length and reverse it. Formally, if $$$a = [a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n]$$$, you can choose any odd integer $$$p$$$ between $$$1$$$ and $$$n$$$, inclusive, and set $$$a$$$ to $$$[a_p, a_{p-1}, \ldots, a_1, a_{p+1}, a_{p+2}, \ldots, a_n]$$$.

Find a way to sort $$$a$$$ using no more than $$$\frac{5n}{2}$$$ reversals of the above kind, or determine that such a way doesn't exist. The number of reversals doesn't have to be minimized.

Time limit: 2s
Memory limit: 512MB

Input:
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 100$$$). Description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $$$n$$$ ($$$3 \le n \le 2021$$$; $$$n$$$ is odd) — the length of the permutation.

The second line contains $$$n$$$ distinct integers $$$a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$$$ ($$$1 \le a_i \le n$$$) — the permutation itself.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$2021$$$.

Output:
For each test case, if it's impossible to sort the given permutation in at most $$$\frac{5n}{2}$$$ reversals, print a single integer $$$-1$$$.

Otherwise, print an integer $$$m$$$ ($$$0 \le m \le \frac{5n}{2}$$$), denoting the number of reversals in your sequence of steps, followed by $$$m$$$ integers $$$p_i$$$ ($$$1 \le p_i \le n$$$; $$$p_i$$$ is odd), denoting the lengths of the prefixes of $$$a$$$ to be reversed, in chronological order.

Note that $$$m$$$ doesn't have to be minimized. If there are multiple answers, print any.

Note:
In the first test case, the permutation is already sorted. Any even number of reversals of the length $$$3$$$ prefix doesn't change that fact.

In the second test case, after reversing the prefix of length $$$3$$$ the permutation will change to $$$[5, 4, 3, 2, 1]$$$, and then after reversing the prefix of length $$$5$$$ the permutation will change to $$$[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]$$$.

In the third test case, it's impossible to sort the permutation.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    3
    3
    1 2 3
    5
    3 4 5 2 1
    3
    2 1 3
    Output:
    4
    3 3 3 3
    2
    3 5
    -1

<think>
Okay so we have a permutation of length n (odd, n ≤ 2021) and we can only reverse odd-length prefixes. The goal is to sort it in at most 5n/2 such reversals. The sum of n over all test cases is at most 2021, so total n across all tests is small. Time limit 2s, memory 512MB.

The hypothesis is BFS on permutation space with parity pruning. I'll represent each permutation as a tuple, use a queue, visited set, and explore all odd p from 1 to n at each step. Prune states where a_i and i have different parity — because reversing an odd-length prefix preserves the parity of positions for elements in the reversed segment? Let me think: reversing an odd-length prefix [1..p] maps position i to p+1-i. The parity of p+1-i: if p is odd, p+1 is even, so p+1-i has opposite parity to i. So elements in the reversed prefix flip parity. Elements after the prefix keep their positions, so parity unchanged. So parity of each element's position can change — the pruning condition "a_i has parity mismatch with i" doesn't hold as an invariant. Actually the sorted array has a_i = i, so parity of a_i equals parity of i. But during reversals, elements can have either parity. So parity pruning as described doesn't make sense as a hard invariant — it's only true for the target, not for all reachable states. I'll skip that pruning and just do plain BFS.

Let me trace on Example 1, first test case: n=3, a=[1,2,3]. Already sorted. BFS starts with queue=[(1,2,3)], visited={(1,2,3)}. Depth 0. Check if target: yes, it's sorted. So m=0, output 0. But the example output says "4\n3 3 3 3" — wait, they output 4 reversals of length 3. The problem says m doesn't have to be minimized, any valid sequence within 5n/2 works. So BFS could find the trivial 0-step solution and output that. That's fine — "print any" means 0 is acceptable. But the example output chose to show 4 steps. So BFS finding 0 is correct.

Second test case: n=5, a=[3,4,5,2,1]. BFS starts. Depth 0: (3,4,5,2,1). Not sorted. Generate all odd p: p=1 → reverse [1] → same. p=3 → reverse first 3: [5,4,3,2,1]. p=5 → reverse all 5: [1,2,5,4,3]. Queue now has these two new states. Depth 1. Check [5,4,3,2,1]: not sorted. Generate: p=1 same, p=3 → reverse first 3 of [5,4,3,2,1] → [3,4,5,2,1] (back to start, visited). p=5 → reverse all → [1,2,3,4,5] — sorted! Found at depth 2. Path: start → p=5 → [1,2,5,4,3] → p=5 → [3,4,5,2,1]? Wait let me trace carefully.

Start: [3,4,5,2,1]. p=5 reverses all: positions 1..5 become reversed → [1,2,5,4,3]. That's state A at depth 1. From state A=[1,2,5,4,3], apply p=5: reverse all → [3,4,5,2,1] back to start. Apply p=3: reverse first 3 → [5,2,1,4,3]. Apply p=1: same. None is sorted. Wait, from [1,2,5,4,3], p=3 gives [5,2,1,4,3]. Not sorted. But the example output is "2\n3 5" — meaning p=3 then p=5. Let me check: start [3,4,5,2,1], p=3 → reverse first 3 → [5,4,3,2,1]. Then p=5 → reverse all 5 → [1,2,3,4,5]. Yes, that's 2 steps. My BFS from start with p=3 gives [5,4,3,2,1] at depth 1. Then from there p=5 gives sorted at depth 2. So BFS finds it. Good.

Third test case: n=3, a=[2,1,3]. BFS: start [2,1,3]. p=1: same. p=3: reverse all → [3,1,2]. Depth 1: [3,1,2]. From [3,1,2]: p=1 same, p=3 → [2,1,3] back. No other states reachable. Neither is sorted. BFS exhausts and returns -1. Matches example.

Now the complexity question. The state space is all permutations reachable from the start via odd-prefix reversals. How many permutations can be reached? For n odd, odd-prefix reversals generate a subgroup of the symmetric group. I don't know the exact size, but it could be large — potentially n!/2 or similar. For n=2021, n! is astronomically huge. BFS would explore an exponential number of states. Even with the depth limit of 5n/2 ≈ 5052 steps, the branching factor at each step is (n+1)/2 ≈ 1011 possible moves. The number of states at depth d is roughly 1011^d, which explodes immediately. BFS cannot work for n beyond tiny values like n≤9 maybe.

But wait — the sum of n over all test cases is ≤ 2021. So there could be one test case with n=2021. BFS on n=2021 is completely impossible — the queue would overflow memory and time long before reaching depth 5052.

The hypothesis of BFS on permutation space is fundamentally infeasible for n up to 2021. The state space is enormous, branching factor is ~n/2, and BFS would explore exponentially many states.

wait — BFS on the full permutation space with n up to 2021 is completely impossible due to factorial state space and branching factor ~1000. Even for n=11, 5^depth explodes.

LEARNING: BFS on permutation space has branching factor ~n/2 ≈ 1010 for n=2021, state space size factorial, making it infeasible beyond n≈9. For n=2021, it would explore exponentially many states and exhaust time/memory instantly.

For each element i from 1 to n-1, I'll find its current position pos, then use prefix reversals to walk it to position i. The idea: reverse prefix of length pos to bring i to index 1, then use a sequence of odd-length prefix reversals to swap it rightward one step at a time until it reaches i.

Let me trace on Example 1's second test case: n=5, a=[3,4,5,2,1]. Target sorted: [1,2,3,4,5].

Start with i=1. Element 1 is at position 5. Reverse prefix of length 5: a becomes [1,2,5,4,3]. Now 1 is at position 1 — already home. No rightward walking needed. Array: [1,2,5,4,3].

i=2. Element 2 is at position 2 — already home. Array: [1,2,5,4,3].

i=3. Element 3 is at position 5. Reverse prefix of length 5: a becomes [3,4,5,2,1]. Now 3 is at position 1. Need to walk it to position 3. Current: [3,4,5,2,1], 3 at index 1.

To swap 3 rightward one step, I need an odd-length prefix reversal that moves 3 from index 1 to index 2. If I reverse prefix of length 3: indices 1..3 become [5,4,3], so array becomes [5,4,3,2,1]. That moved 3 to index 3 — two steps right, not one. Hmm. Let me think more carefully.

Actually, let me reconsider the walking mechanism. If 3 is at index 1 and I want it at index 2, I could: reverse prefix of length 3 to put 3 at index 3, then reverse prefix of length 3 again? No, that just flips back. 

Let me try a different sequence. Starting from [3,4,5,2,1] with 3 at index 1, target index 3:
- Reverse prefix length 3: [5,4,3,2,1]. 3 is now at index 3. Done for i=3 in one move? But wait — the suffix from index 4 onward should already be sorted for i=1,2. Currently suffix [2,1] is not sorted. The approach assumes suffix a[i+1..n] is sorted, but after bringing 3 home, the suffix got scrambled.

Let me restart the trace more carefully, processing i from n down to 1 (largest elements first), since that's more natural for building a sorted suffix.

Actually, the hypothesis says "outer loop decreasing i, treating suffix a[i+1..n] as sorted." So process i from n down to 1.

n=5, a=[3,4,5,2,1]. Start with i=5. Element 5 is at position 3. Reverse prefix length 3: [5,4,3,2,1]. Now 5 is at index 1. Need to walk it to index 5. 

To move 5 rightward: reverse prefix length 3 → [3,4,5,2,1] (5 at index 3). Then reverse prefix length 5 → [1,2,5,4,3] (5 at index 3 still? Wait: reversing length 5 on [3,4,5,2,1] gives [1,2,5,4,3] — 5 is at index 3). Then reverse prefix length 3 → [5,2,1,4,3] (5 at index 1). This is oscillating. 

The problem: a single odd-length prefix reversal can only move an element from position p to position (odd_length - p + 1) within the prefix. To move an element rightward by exactly 1 position, I'd need a reversal that places it at p+1, which means odd_length - p + 1 = p+1 → odd_length = 2p. But 2p is even when p is any integer. So odd_length would need to be even — impossible. 

Wait — let me check: if element is at position 1, reversing prefix length 3 puts it at position 3 (moves right by 2). Reversing prefix length 5 puts it at position 5 (moves right by 4). An element at position 1 can only jump to odd positions: 1,3,5,7,... It can never land on an even index via a single prefix reversal from index 1. 

So if I bring element i to index 1, I can only place it at odd indices thereafter. But its target position i could be even. For n=5, target positions: 5 is odd (ok), 4 is even (not ok), 3 is odd (ok), 2 is even (not ok), 1 is odd (ok). 

oh wait — from index 1, odd-length reversals can only place the element at odd indices. Target positions 2 and 4 are unreachable from index 1.

LEARNING: Element at index 1 can only be moved to odd indices via odd-length prefix reversals; even target positions are unreachable from the front.

Hmm, all those ideas fell apart. BFS was impossible, greedy swaps couldn't reach even positions, and recursive median placement broke on parity constraints.

Wait — that parity constraint is the key. Under an odd-length prefix reversal, an element moves from position i to position p+1-i. Since p is odd, p+1 is even, so parity(i) = parity(p+1-i). The parity of each element's position is invariant! So element 1 must end at position 1 (odd), element 2 at position 2 (even), and so on. If the permutation violates this — if any element is in a position of opposite parity — it's immediately impossible. That's why Example 3 (2 1 3) fails: 2 is at odd index 1, but needs even index 2.

So for possible permutations, we must fix from the back. But how? If I try to place n at position n, a reversal with p=n moves it to front, not back. hmm. What if I place two numbers at once? n and n-1. Their target positions are n (odd) and n-1 (even). I can first bring n to the front (p = position_of_n, which is odd because parity is invariant, so n is at an odd index). Reverse that prefix: now n is at index 1. Then bring n-1 to position 2? Its current index is even. I can reverse a prefix ending at n-1's position? No, that would move n away. Wait — what if I reverse a prefix of length position_of_n-1? That's even, not allowed. Oh, but I can first move n-1 near the front. Let me think stepwise.

Take Example 2: [3,4,5,2,1]. n=5. Parity check: 1 at pos5 (odd, needs odd) ✓, 2 at pos4 (even, needs even) ✓, etc. All good. Now place 5 and 4. 5 is at index 3 (odd). Reverse p=3: array becomes [5,4,3,2,1]. Now 5 is at index1, 4 at index2. That's perfect! Their targets are index5 and 4. Need to send them to the back. I can reverse p=5: [1,2,3,4,5]. Done. That's two moves: 3 and 5. The example output says 2 moves: 3 and 5. Exactly!

But what if they aren't adjacent after the first move? Suppose n is at some odd index i, n-1 at some even index j. After reversing prefix i, n goes to front. Where does n-1 go? Its new index is i+1-j? Let's derive a general procedure. I need to get n to front, n-1 to second position, then reverse the whole array to place them at the end. So after first reversal, I need n-1 at index 2. That means before that reversal, n-1 must be at index i-1 (since reversal of prefix i maps index i-1 to index 2). But i is odd, i-1 is even — good, n-1's parity is even. So I must first move n-1 to position i-1. How? Its current index j is even. I can reverse prefix of length j-1? That's odd if j is even. Let's test: if n-1 is at even j, reverse prefix j-1 (odd) will bring it to position j-1? Actually, reversal of prefix p maps element at original index x to new index p+1-x. If I want to move n-1 from j to i-1, I need p such that p+1-j = i-1 ⇒ p = i+j-2. p must be odd. i is odd, j is even, so i+j is odd, minus 2 remains odd. Good! So one reversal can place n-1 at i-1. Then reverse prefix i to bring n to front and n-1 to index 2. Then reverse whole array (p=n) to send them to the end. That's three moves per pair. But wait, after moving n-1 to i-1, the position of n might shift? n is at i, which is > i-1, so after reversing prefix p=i+j-2, n's index becomes p+1-i = (i+j-2)+1-i = j-1. That's not i anymore. So the next reversal's prefix length must be based on n's new position. This is getting messy.

Let me trace on a harder case: n=7, a=[2,6,4,7,3,5,1]. Parity check passes. First pair: 7 and 6. 7 at index4 (even? Wait, 4 is even, but 7 needs odd index — contradiction! Actually parity invariant says 7 must be at odd index initially. So this example is invalid. Let's make a valid one: a=[7,2,4,6,3,5,1]. 7 at index1 (odd), 6 at index4 (even). i=1, j=4. To place n-1 at i-1=0? That's impossible. So if n is already at front, i=1, i-1=0, so n-1 must be brought to index2 directly. Its current j=4. Reverse prefix of length j-1=3 (odd): a becomes [4,2,7,6,3,5,1]. Now 6 is at index4 still. Not at index2. That didn't work. I need a different approach.

Maybe I should bring n-1 to index2 first, then bring n to index1. But n-1 at even j, to get to index2, I could reverse prefix of length j (even? not allowed) or j-1? Let's calculate: to move element from j to 2 via reversal of prefix p, we need p+1-j=2 ⇒ p=j+1. p must be odd. j is even, so j+1 is odd. Good! So reverse prefix p=j+1. That moves n-1 to index2. But where does n go? n is at odd i. After this reversal, n's new index is p+1-i = (j+1)+1-i = j+2-i. That could be anything. Then I can bring n to front by reversing prefix of length new position of n (which is odd because parity preserved). Then both are at front, reverse whole array. That's three moves again.

Let me test on earlier [3,4,5,2,1] with this 3-move method. n=5 at i=3, n-1=4 at j=2 (even). Step1: bring n-1 to index2: p=j+1=3. Reverse prefix 3: [5,4,3,2,1]. Now 4 is at index2 (good), 5 is at index1 (already front!). Step2: bring n to front — it's already there. Step3: reverse whole array p=5: [1,2,3,4,5]. That's 2 moves actually, because step1 already placed n at front. So sometimes it takes 2, sometimes 3.

Now test a case needing 3 moves. Take n=7, a=[4,2,7,6,3,5,1]. 7 at i=3 (odd), 6 at j=4 (even). Step1: bring 6 to index2: p=j+1=5. Reverse prefix 5: array becomes [3,6,7,2,4,5,1]. Now 6 at index2 (good), 7 at index3. Step2: bring 7 to front: p=3. Reverse prefix 3: [7,6,3,2,4,5,1]. Step3: reverse whole array p=7: [1,5,4,2,3,6,7]. Now 7 and 6 are at the end. But the middle is unsorted. We continue on next pair: now treat n=5 and 4. Current array: [1,5,4,2,3,6,7]. 5 at index2 (even — needs odd? Wait, 5's target is index5 (odd). Currently at even index2 — parity mismatch? But parity is invariant under operations we performed, and initially all parities matched. Let's check initial: [4,2,7,6,3,5,1]. 5 at index6 (even, target index5 odd? Actually target for 5 is index5, which is odd. But initial index6 is even — mismatch! So this example is invalid. Right, parity must hold initially. So let's construct a valid permutation: start with sorted [1,2,3,4,5,6,7] and apply some odd-length prefix reversals to shuffle while preserving parity. Apply p=3: [3,2,1,4,5,6,7]. Then p=5: [5,4,1,2,3,6,7]. This is valid. Now 7 at i=6 (even? 6 is even, but 7 needs odd — so invalid again! Because p=5 reversal: original index of 7 was 7 (odd), after reversal p=5, new index = 5+1-7 = -1? Wait, formula only for indices ≤ p. 7 is index7 > p=5, so it stays at index7. So 7 remains at odd index7. Good. So array is [5,4,1,2,3,6,7]. 7 at i=7 (odd), 6 at i=6 (even). Pair 7 and 6 are already at the end! So move to next pair 5 and 4. 5 at i=1 (odd), 4 at i=2 (even). They are at front. Reverse whole array p=7? That would move 7 and 6 away. Instead, I need to send 5 and 4 to positions 5 and 4. They are currently at 1 and 2. I can reverse prefix of length 5? That's odd. Reverse p=5: array becomes [3,2,1,4,5,6,7]. Now 5 at index5, 4 at index4 — perfect! So one move fixed that pair. Then next pair 3 and 2: currently at indices 1 and 2. Need positions 3 and 2. 2 is already at index2. Need to move 3 to index3. Reverse p=3: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7]. Done.

So algorithm: check parity condition. If fail, output -1. Else, for k from n down to 3 step 2: place k and k-1 at positions k and k-1. They are currently somewhere. If they are already in place, skip. If not, if k is at position pos_k (odd), k-1 at pos_{k-1} (even). We want them at the end. Strategy: first ensure k-1 is at index 2. If not, reverse prefix of length pos_{k-1}+1 (odd) to bring it to index 2. Then bring k to index 1 by reversing prefix of length pos_k (odd) — but pos_k may have changed after first reversal, so we need to find its new position. Then reverse prefix of length k (odd) to send the pair to the end. That's at most 3 moves per pair. Over n/2 pairs, that's ≤ 3n/2 moves, well under 5n/2. But wait, after we send them to the end, the middle part is of length k-2, and we continue. The moves we make might disturb already placed tail? No, because reversing prefix of length k only touches first k elements, and the tail from k+1 to n is already sorted and remains unchanged. Good.

Let's trace Example 2 fully with this method. n=5, a=[3,4,5,2,1]. Pair 5 and 4. pos_5=3 (odd), pos_4=2 (even). k-1=4 is already at index2? pos_4=2, yes. So skip first step. Bring k to index1: reverse prefix length pos_5=3 → [5,4,3,2,1]. Now reverse prefix length k=5 → [1,2,3,4,5]. That's 2 moves: 3 and 5. Matches output.

Now Example 1 first test case: n=3, a=[1,2,3]. Pair 3 and 2. pos_3=3 (odd), pos_2=2 (even). k-1=2 already at index2. Bring k to index1: reverse prefix length 3 → [3,2,1]. Then reverse prefix length 3 → [1,2,3]. That's 2 moves. But example output says 4 moves: 3 3 3 3. They used a different sequence, but any sequence works. Our sequence is valid and within limit (2 ≤ 5*3/2=7.5). So we can output ours.

Edge case: what if during the process, after bringing k-1 to index2, k is already at index1? Then skip the second reversal. So at most 3, sometimes 2, sometimes 0 moves per pair.

Now verify the parity condition on Example 3: n=3, a=[2,1,3]. 1 at index2 (even, needs odd) → fail. Output -1. Correct.

aha! — so the invariant gives the impossibility condition, and the pairwise back-to-front placement with at most 3 moves per pair fits within 5n/2 easily. The key was realizing that you can always maneuver the two largest numbers to the front in two moves (or three if they're not cooperative) and then one move to send them to the back, without disturbing the already-sorted suffix.
</think>
<code>
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

void solve() {
    ll n;
    cin >> n;
    vll a(n);
    for (ll i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        cin >> a[i];
        a[i]--;
    }
    vll res;
    while (n > 1) {
        ll x = n - 2;
        ll y = n - 1;
        ll lx = 0;
        ll ly = 0;
        auto set_val = [&]() {
            lx = 0;
            ly = 0;
            while (a[lx] != x) lx++;
            while (a[ly] != y) ly++;
        };
        set_val();
        if (((x - lx) & 1) || ((y - ly) & 1)) {
            cout << -1 << '\n';
            return;
        }
        res.pb(ly + 1);
        reverse(a.begin(), a.begin() + ly + 1);
        set_val();
        res.pb(lx);
        reverse(a.begin(), a.begin() + lx);
        set_val();
        res.pb(lx + 2);
        reverse(a.begin(), a.begin() + lx + 2);
        set_val();
        res.pb(3);
        reverse(a.begin(), a.begin() + 3);
        res.pb(n);
        reverse(a.begin(), a.begin() + n);
        n -= 2;
    }
    cout << sz(res) << '\n';
    for (ll r : res) {
        cout << r << ' ';
    }
    cout << '\n';
}

int main() {
    fastio;
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}
</code>

For each element i from 1 to n-1, I'll find its current position pos, then use prefix reversals to walk it to position i. The idea: reverse prefix of length pos to bring i to index 1, then use a sequence of odd-length prefix reversals to swap it rightward one step at a time until it reaches i.

Wait — that parity constraint is the key. Under an odd-length prefix reversal, an element moves from position i to position p+1-i. Since p is odd, p+1 is even, so parity(i) = parity(p+1-i). Let's calculate: to move element from j to 2 via reversal of prefix p, we need p+1-j=2 ⇒ p=j+1. p must be odd. j is even, so j+1 is odd. Good! So reverse prefix p=j+1. That moves n-1 to index2. Then I can bring n to front by reversing prefix of length new position of n (which is odd because parity preserved). Then both are at front, reverse whole array. That's three moves again.

Because p=5 reversal: original index of 7 was 7 (odd), after reversal p=5, new index = 5+1-7 = -1? Wait, formula only for indices ≤ p. 7 is index7 > p=5, so it stays at index7. Our sequence is valid and within limit (2 ≤ 5*3/2=7.5). So we can output ours.

Now verify the parity condition on Example 3: n=3, a=[2,1,3]. 1 at index2 (even, needs odd) → fail. Output -1. Correct.

aha! — so the invariant gives the impossibility condition, and the pairwise back-to-front placement with at most 3 moves per pair fits within 5n/2 easily. The key was realizing that you can always maneuver the two largest numbers to the front in two moves (or three if they're not cooperative) and then one move to send them to the back, without disturbing the already-sorted suffix.

finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to determine if we can sort a permutation of distinct integers from 1 to n using at most \( \frac{5n}{2} \) reversals of prefixes with odd lengths. Let's break down the approach: ### Key Observations: 1. **Permutation Properties**: The permutation consists of distinct integers from 1 to n. 2. **Odd Length Prefix Reversal**: We can reverse any odd-length prefix. This means we can swap elements within a segment of the permutation. 3. **Sorting Requirement**: We need to sort the permutation in increasing order. 4. **Constraint on Reversals**: The total number of reversals should be ≤ \( \frac{5n}{2} \). ### Strategy: 1. **Initial Check**: If the permutation is already sorted, return 0 reversals. 2. **General Approach**: - For each element in the permutation, find its correct position in the sorted permutation. - Calculate the distance to move the current element to its correct position. - Use prefix reversals to move the element closer to its target position. - Track the number of reversals used. ### Implementation Details: 1. **Track Moves**: We need to keep track of how many moves we make for each element to reach its correct position. 2. **Prefix Reversals**: Each reversal affects a contiguous segment of the permutation. We need to carefully select segments to minimize the number of reversals. 3. **Edge Cases**: - If an element is already in the correct position, skip it. - Ensure that we always pick the smallest possible prefix length that allows us to move the element towards its target. ### Complexity Analysis: - Time: O(n^2) in the worst case due to nested loops for finding positions and moving elements. - Memory: O(1) extra space, just for tracking indices and counts. ### Validation with Examples: Let's validate our approach with the provided examples. #### Example 1: - Permutation: [1, 2, 3] - Already sorted, so answer is 0. #### Example 2: - Permutation: [3, 4, 5, 2, 1] - Target: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] - Moves needed: - Move 3 to index 0: Reverse prefix of length 3 → [5, 4, 3, 2, 1] - Move 5 to index 2: Reverse prefix of length 5 → [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] - Total reversals: 2. #### Example 3: - Permutation: [2, 1, 3] - Target: [1, 2, 3] - Moves needed: - Move 2 to index 0: Reverse prefix of length 3 → [3, 1, 2] - Move 3 to index 2: Reverse prefix of length 3 → [2, 1, 3] - Total reversals: 2. Our approach seems reasonable. Now, let's implement this in C++. #include #include #include using namespace std; void solve(int n, vector& a) { int moves = 0; vector visited(n + 1, false); for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) { if (visited[i]) continue; int pos = i; while (!visited[a[pos]]) { visited[a[pos]] = true; pos = a[pos]; ++moves; } } if (moves > (5 * n / 2)) { cout << "-1" << endl; return; } cout << moves << endl; for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) { if (a[i] == i) continue; int len = 1; while (len % 2 == 0) ++len; cout << len << " "; } cout << endl; } int main() { ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int t; cin >> t; while (t--) { int n; cin >> n; vector a(n + 1); for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) { cin >> a[i]; } solve(n, a); } return 0; }

To solve this problem, we need to determine if we can sort a permutation of distinct integers from 1 to n using at most \( \frac{5n}{2} \) reversals of prefixes with odd lengths. Let's break down the approach:

### Key Observations:
1. **Permutation Properties**: The permutation consists of distinct integers from 1 to n.
2. **Odd Length Prefix Reversal**: We can reverse any odd-length prefix. This means we can swap elements within a segment of the permutation.
3. **Sorting Requirement**: We need to sort the permutation in increasing order.
4. **Constraint on Reversals**: The total number of reversals should be ≤ \( \frac{5n}{2} \).

### Strategy:
1. **Initial Check**: If the permutation is already sorted, return 0 reversals.
2. **General Approach**:
   - For each element in the permutation, find its correct position in the sorted permutation.
   - Calculate the distance to move the current element to its correct position.
   - Use prefix reversals to move the element closer to its target position.
   - Track the number of reversals used.

### Implementation Details:
1. **Track Moves**: We need to keep track of how many moves we make for each element to reach its correct position.
2. **Prefix Reversals**: Each reversal affects a contiguous segment of the permutation. We need to carefully select segments to minimize the number of reversals.
3. **Edge Cases**:
   - If an element is already in the correct position, skip it.
   - Ensure that we always pick the smallest possible prefix length that allows us to move the element towards its target.

### Complexity Analysis:
- Time: O(n^2) in the worst case due to nested loops for finding positions and moving elements.
- Memory: O(1) extra space, just for tracking indices and counts.

### Validation with Examples:
Let's validate our approach with the provided examples.

#### Example 1:
- Permutation: [1, 2, 3]
- Already sorted, so answer is 0.

#### Example 2:
- Permutation: [3, 4, 5, 2, 1]
- Target: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
- Moves needed: 
  - Move 3 to index 0: Reverse prefix of length 3 → [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]
  - Move 5 to index 2: Reverse prefix of length 5 → [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
  - Total reversals: 2.

#### Example 3:
- Permutation: [2, 1, 3]
- Target: [1, 2, 3]
- Moves needed:
  - Move 2 to index 0: Reverse prefix of length 3 → [3, 1, 2]
  - Move 3 to index 2: Reverse prefix of length 3 → [2, 1, 3]
  - Total reversals: 2.

Our approach seems reasonable. Now, let's implement this in C++.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
Consider a sequence of integers $$$a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$$$. In one move, you can select any element of the sequence and delete it. After an element is deleted, all elements to the right are shifted to the left by $$$1$$$ position, so there are no empty spaces in the sequence. So after you make a move, the sequence's length decreases by $$$1$$$. The indices of the elements after the move are recalculated.

E. g. let the sequence be $$$a=[3, 2, 2, 1, 5]$$$. Let's select the element $$$a_3=2$$$ in a move. Then after the move the sequence will be equal to $$$a=[3, 2, 1, 5]$$$, so the $$$3$$$-rd element of the new sequence will be $$$a_3=1$$$ and the $$$4$$$-th element will be $$$a_4=5$$$.

You are given a sequence $$$a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$$$ and a number $$$k$$$. You need to find the minimum number of moves you have to make so that in the resulting sequence there will be at least $$$k$$$ elements that are equal to their indices, i. e. the resulting sequence $$$b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_m$$$ will contain at least $$$k$$$ indices $$$i$$$ such that $$$b_i = i$$$.

Time limit: 1s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
The first line contains one integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 100$$$) — the number of test cases. Then $$$t$$$ test cases follow.

Each test case consists of two consecutive lines. The first line contains two integers $$$n$$$ and $$$k$$$ ($$$1 \le k \le n \le 2000$$$). The second line contains a sequence of integers $$$a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$$$ ($$$1 \le a_i \le n$$$). The numbers in the sequence are not necessarily different.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ over all test cases doesn't exceed $$$2000$$$.

Output:
For each test case output in a single line:

- $$$-1$$$ if there's no desired move sequence;
- otherwise, the integer $$$x$$$ ($$$0 \le x \le n$$$) — the minimum number of the moves to be made so that the resulting sequence will contain at least $$$k$$$ elements that are equal to their indices.

Note:
In the first test case the sequence doesn't satisfy the desired condition, but it can be provided by deleting the first element, hence the sequence will be $$$[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]$$$ and $$$6$$$ elements will be equal to their indices.

In the second test case there are two ways to get the desired result in $$$2$$$ moves: the first one is to delete the $$$1$$$-st and the $$$3$$$-rd elements so that the sequence will be $$$[1, 2, 3]$$$ and have $$$3$$$ elements equal to their indices; the second way is to delete the $$$2$$$-nd and the $$$3$$$-rd elements to get the sequence $$$[5, 2, 3]$$$ with $$$2$$$ desired elements.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    4
    7 6
    1 1 2 3 4 5 6
    5 2
    5 1 3 2 3
    5 2
    5 5 5 5 4
    8 4
    1 2 3 3 2 2 5 5
    Output:
    1
    2
    -1
    2

reference.full:
<think>
Okay so we have an array of n integers, and we can delete elements one by one, shifting everything left so indices get recalculated. <code>
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

const int M = 1000000007;
const int MM = 998244353;

template<typename T, typename U> static inline void amin(T &x, U y){ if(y<x) x=y; }
template<typename T, typename U> static inline void amax(T &x, U y){ if(x<y) x=y; }

void solve()
{
    ll n,k;
    cin >> n >> k;
    vll a(n+1);
    for(ll i=1;i<=n;++i)
        cin >> a[i];
    vector<vll> dp(n+10,vll(n+10,0));
    dp[0][0] = 0;
    for(ll i=0;i<n;++i)
    {
        for(ll j=0;j<=i;++j)
        {
            amax(dp[i+1][j],dp[i][j]);
            if(a[i+1] == j + 1)
                amax(dp[i+1][j+1],dp[i][j] + 1);
            else
                amax(dp[i+1][j+1],dp[i][j]);
        }
    }
    ll ans = -1;
    for(ll i=1;i<=n;++i)
    {
        if(dp[n][i] >= k)
            amax(ans,i);
    }
    if(ans == -1)
        cout << ans << "\n";
    else
        cout << n - ans << "\n";
}

int main()
{
    fastio;
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--)
        solve();
    return 0;
}

Therefore, the algorithm is: For each index i from 1 to n, if a[i] <= i, consider it as a candidate. Then find the longest strictly increasing subsequence of a[i] values among these candidates. Let L be its length. If L < k, output -1. Else, output n - L.

DP[i] = length of longest strictly increasing subsequence ending at candidate i, where we only consider candidates in order of index. Since indices are increasing, we can just process in order. For each candidate i, DP[i] = 1 + max{DP[j] for j < i and a[j] < a[i] and a[j] <= j}. That's straightforward.
